cards.text: At least 42 people have died in landslides linked to projects that bypassed or violated environmental conditions. The mechanism is constitutional — and the reform that would fix it has been refused by most states for three years.
cards.sub: Malaysia's Federal Constitution does not define 'environment.' Land and forestry sit in the State List. The federal Department of Environment can object to developments — but state planning authorities can override those objections. In Tanjung Bungah (2017), 11 construction workers died after a hillside project proceeded despite DOE rejection. In Batang Kali (2022), 31 people died at a campsite the ministry said was operating in a designated No-Go Area in breach of EIA conditions. Parliament passed forest protection reforms in September 2022. Three years later, only Perlis and the Federal Territories have fully adopted them.

cards.h: The word 'environment' is absent from the Federal Constitution. DOE can object — but state planning authorities can proceed regardless.
cards.s: Under the 9th Schedule, land and forestry sit in the State List. The Concurrent List covers wildlife protection and town planning. But 'environment' has no constitutional definition or jurisdictional home. The Environmental Quality Act 1974 is federal law, but DOE approves EIA reports — not projects. When DOE rejects a development, state One Stop Centre committees can override the rejection. Professor Ainul Jaria Maidin documents: planning authorities 'are not required to strictly follow the opinions of other authorities and may choose to ignore the recommendations of the DOE.' The National Forestry Act 1984 was enacted under Article 76(1)(b) to promote uniformity, but under Article 76(3), it does not operate in a state unless adopted there. Parliament passed the NFA Amendment Act 2022 with mandatory public inquiries and replacement forest requirements. As of mid-2025, only Perlis and the Federal Territories have fully adopted it. Selangor has a separate 2011 state-level public inquiry requirement — which is not the same as adopting the 2022 federal amendment. Negeri Sembilan moved to adopt in August 2025. The states with the highest recorded forest loss — including Kelantan, Kedah, and Pahang — have not adopted.
cards.d: Sources: Federal Constitution 9th Schedule, Article 76(1)(b)/(3), Prof. Ainul Jaria Maidin/Malaysian Bar, SAM/FOE Malaysia (Mar 2025), Bernama (May 2024 — Perlis first), The Sun (2025 — adoption status). CORRECTED: Article citation fixed from 94(1) to 76(1)(b); NFA adoption status corrected; Selangor's distinct legal position acknowledged.

cards.h: Satellite estimates of forest loss far exceed official figures — and the gap reveals a governance blind spot, not just a measurement problem.
cards.s: Between 2001 and 2019, satellite-based estimates recorded over 725,000 hectares of primary forest loss in Peninsular Malaysia, while official forestry statistics recorded approximately 245,000 hectares of forest area lost over a similar period. These are different metrics — primary forest loss versus total forest area change — so the comparison is not a clean multiple. But the scale of the gap raises questions about what official statistics capture. Johor's 2014 excision of 15,011 hectares from Jemaluang and Tenggaroh — the largest one-time excision in Peninsular Malaysia since 2000 — was described by Macaranga as 'carried out secretly,' with the state forestry department unable to justify it to certification auditors. About 21% of the excised area had already been cleared, on land owned by Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar. Kedah resumed logging in the 160,000-hectare Ulu Muda water catchment despite a 2003 federal ban, classifying the majority as 'production forest.' Ulu Muda supplies over 80% of Penang's water. In Sabah and Sarawak, which hold the majority of Malaysia's forest cover, different forestry statutes and native customary rights under MA63 create distinct — and in some cases more severe — deforestation dynamics that this Peninsular-focused analysis does not fully capture.
cards.d: Sources: Macaranga/Pulitzer Center (Johor excision), Mongabay (2022 — Ulu Muda), RimbaWatch/Borneo Project (2024), SCMP/Pulitzer Center (Johor royal connection), GFW. CORRECTED: removed false 3x claim; acknowledged metric differences; added East Malaysia caveat; qualified RimbaWatch year-on-year comparison.

cards.h: Parliament passed forest reforms in 2022. The states that cut the most forest have not adopted them — and the incentives explain why.
cards.s: The NFA Amendment Act 2022 mandates public inquiry before excision and replacement forest of equal or greater size. Three years later, adoption is minimal. States resist not from ideology but from revenue logic: logging royalties, land premiums, and development charges fund state budgets directly. Kelantan recorded approximately 8,400 hectares of natural forest loss in 2024. Kedah continues logging Ulu Muda. Pahang's bauxite boom operated without any EIA, generating RM46.7 million in royalties in 2015 alone while contaminating coastline. The pattern crosses party lines — PAS-governed Kelantan, BN-linked Pahang, and PH-allied Selangor all exploit the same constitutional gap. Forest management certification was suspended for Kelantan, Kedah, and Johor — though Johor later regained limited-scope certification. But certification is voluntary with no legal consequence. The 11 construction workers who died in Tanjung Bungah and the 31 who died at Batang Kali were overwhelmingly low-income and migrant workers — the people with the least power to challenge the approvals that put them in harm's way. States need land revenue because federal fiscal transfers don't compensate conservation. Ecological Fiscal Transfers exist but remain too small to change the calculus.
cards.d: Sources: SAM/FOE Malaysia (Mar 2025 — adoption), GFW (Kelantan 2024), Wikipedia/Malaysian Insight (Pahang bauxite), Macaranga (certification), MTCC (Johor regained limited scope). CORRECTED: Kelantan figure to 8.4k ha; Johor certification qualified; added class dimension of landslide victims; added fiscal incentive explanation.

cards.h: This isn't development versus environment. It's a system where states profit from land conversion and the federal government cannot stop them — and the people who die are never the people who approve.
cards.text: Every case follows the same pattern: state approves project on state land; DOE objects or is bypassed; state proceeds. The Tanjung Bungah workers were construction labourers. The Batang Kali victims were families on holiday. The Orang Asli communities displaced by forest excision had no representation in the planning process. The developers and political operators who benefit face no consequence when projects kill people. India's Forest Rights Act gives indigenous communities standing regardless of when decisions were made. South Africa's Constitution includes a justiciable environmental right. Indonesia's Omnibus Law weakened environmental protections to attract investment — showing the alternative isn't always better. What Malaysia has is a middle ground that protects no one: federal environmental law without enforcement power, state land authority without accountability, and voluntary reforms that the states most in need refuse to adopt.

Malaysia's environmental governance has a constitutional gap at its centre. The word 'environment' is absent from the Federal Constitution. Land is a state matter. DOE can advise but not veto. Parliament can pass reforms but cannot compel adoption. The NFA Amendment Act 2022 was the right reform — mandatory public inquiry, mandatory replacement forest. Three years later, only two jurisdictions have fully adopted it. The states with the highest forest loss have not, because land conversion generates revenue that federal transfers do not replace. At least 42 people have died in landslides linked to projects that bypassed or violated environmental conditions — overwhelmingly low-income and migrant workers. Official forest-loss figures substantially undercount satellite-measured reality. What Malaysia needs is not another voluntary framework but a structural fix: either a constitutional amendment placing environment under the Federal List, giving DOE actual enforcement power; or fiscal transfers large enough to make conservation more profitable than conversion for state governments. The first requires two-thirds parliamentary majority and state consent. The second requires federal money. Neither is politically easy. But every year without either, the pattern repeats: states approve, DOE objects, development proceeds, and the political cost is borne by people who never had a voice in the decision.

sources: Federal Constitution 9th Schedule, Article 76(1)(b)/(3), Prof. Ainul Jaria Maidin/Malaysian Bar, SAM/FOE Malaysia, Macaranga/Pulitzer Center, Mongabay, RimbaWatch/Borneo Project, DOE Malaysia, MTCC, GFW, Bernama, The Sun, Eco-Business, SCMP/Pulitzer Center, The Malaysian Insight (Pahang bauxite)
confidence: High on constitutional structure (legal text verified). High on Tanjung Bungah deaths and DOE rejection (multiple sources). Medium-High on NFA adoption status (Bernama, The Sun, corrected). Medium on Batang Kali EIA breach link (ministry statement, but official report attributes landslide to geology). Medium on forest-loss figures (different metrics acknowledged). Medium on bauxite royalties (media reporting).
